The `layers.lock` file seems a very convenient approach. If I understand this correctly in the `layers.lock` you would be able to say
1. "get layer L from the head of branch B", or
2. "get layer L from latest tag"
3. "get layer L from tag C.V.X with X being the maximum you can find"
4. "get layer L from tag C.X.Y with X and Y being the maximum you can find"
5. "get layer L from commit G"

During a charm build you would clone the layer L and point it to the desired branch/tag/commit.
It is only option 5 that pins the code and it is something we do not want to promote. Given we have https://github.com/juju/charm-tools/issues/405 to recreate a past build I do not mind if we do not have option 5 at all.

I would downvote the idea of introducing the concept of channels since
1. Tags and branches (marked as stable/unstable/featureX etc) are what people are used to express code maturity. I do not see the point of reinventing that.
2. Channels alone are not enough, we should also have tracks that would map to backwards incompatible/future releases of a layer.
3. It would add more complexity to our index.

It is true that we can support easily vendoring through `$JUJU_REPOSITORY`, `$LAYER_PATH` and `$INTERFACE_PATH`  but we don't do so officialy. We could diconnect the dependency management steps from the build process. We could expect the user to update the dependencies via a `layers.lock` file right before she charm builds.

The problem is actually that [`Path.open()`](https://pythonhosted.org/path.py/api.html#path.Path.open) is returning a [`TextIOWrapper`](https://docs.python.org/2/library/io.html#io.TextIOWrapper) instead of a standard file handle, which is unicode only:

```python
>>> open('/tmp/foo', 'w').write('foo')
>>> open(Path('/tmp/foo'), 'w').write('foo')
>>> Path('/tmp/foo').open('w').write('foo')
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
TypeError: must be unicode, not str
```

Should be a simple [one line](https://github.com/juju/charm-tools/blob/master/charmtools/build/tactics.py#L321) fix.

Actually, I think the workaround described is appropriate enough. I'd probably just have it be this though:

```python
service = cls.deployment.charm_name
# Specify charm_name because this layer could be named something else.
cls.deployment.add(service, units=3)

for unit in self.deployment.sentry[service]
    # Use the individual units in this block.
```

Either way, naming is the same as reactive file naming. So all you need to do is name the test `10_tls_deploy.py` or really just `tls_deploy.py`. Preface the tests with the layer name to avoid collisions.

@aaosssa Ubuntu 14.04 LTS has outdated apt packages for both requests (2.2.1) and urllib3 (1.7.1).  In my case, I was using salt to configure an instance and the docker salt formula attempts to install `docker-py`.

In the following line, the `requests` import fails, resulting in an attempt to import `urllib3` directly.  

```python
try:
    import requests.packages.urllib3 as urllib3
except ImportError:
    import urllib3
```

Then, the following line fails because it successfully imported older version of `urllib3`.

```python
urllib3.connection.match_hostname = match_hostname
```

Another reasonable approach (IMHO) is to not attempt to gracefully recover from the failed import and just let it fail.  That would also avoid adding a dependency.

Ok, that somewhat explains things ;-). You have _two_ loops, and both loop over a result set from the database. The thing is, when you call `execute()`, any old result sets are discarded. Once you finish the tables loop inside the connections loop, the result set will be finished. When you try to continue with the next iteration in the connections loop, the first result set is gone, too. Hence the error message you see. A `fetchall()` in the connections loop would indeed fix that.

Side remark: Since `execute()` returns the `Cursor` object, it is probably misleading to assign the Cursor object to two other variables.

So, you're right that `Server` is supposed to be the main entry point. It will run `DiscoveryProtocol`, `PeerPool`, authenticate incoming peer connections (which should eventually be moved into its separate service), and run the service responsible for chain sync. 

Now, for the chain sync service, my plan is to have only `FullNodeSyncer` and `LightNodeSyncer`, where the latter will be factored out from the existing `LightChain` (or even from `FullNodeSyncer`). These are supposed to make sure we're always in sync, but won't provide APIs for chain/state data retrieval themselves -- that would stay in the current `Chain` service offered by `trinity` (with possibly a separate one that can fetch data on-demand, for when we're in light mode)

Finally, to clarify the state of the current `*Syncer` classes:
 - `ChainSyncer`: does the chain part of fast-sync, fetching/storing headers, bodies and receipts 
 - `StateDownloader`: does the state part of fast-sync, fetching/storing the whole state trie starting from a given root hash
 - `RegularChainSyncer`: does the regular sync, fetching headers/bodies and importing them
 
I'm thinking of renaming the first to `FastChainSyncer`, but those are all wrapped by `FullNodeSyncer` anyway.

Hi.

Excuse me: the laptop is not with Windows 10 but with Windows 7.

I was able to resolve this issue by following a set of simple steps I came across on another similar thread on Github. Here are the steps that worked for me:-

1. Open anaconda prompt window (preferably launched as Administrator)
2. Run this command
       **conda update conda**
3. then run,
        **conda update ipykernel**
4 and finally :
**conda update --all**

Original post: https://github.com/ContinuumIO/anaconda-issues/issues/8087#issuecomment-365397764

* https://github.com/conda/conda/pull/5937 Is potentially relevant here, and likely coming in conda 4.4. Conda 4.3 and prior has a *ton* of churn in package caches, especially when switching between "full ecosystem" channels like `defaults` and `conda-forge`. That churn makes the global package cache concept pretty much unworkable. The #5937 PR should at least make the situation workable, and if we add an additional hash to file names as proposed in a comment, make the shared package cache concept solid (maybe with some of the safeguards you've proposed).

* If you're worried about disk space and want to use a shared package cache, *make sure* the package cache is on the same device mount as where your environments will be, so that hardlinks can be created between the cache and the environment. (It's obvious for people who know the technical details here, but worth repeating for those who don't.)

* Pre-link scripts mutate a package after it's extracted into a package cache, but before the package is linked into an environment. They are an abomination and should be outlawed completely. Actually I could add aconfiguration parameter that prevents them from running. The most noticeable hit would be the old-style python noarch packages.

* We'll be adding back some type of locking to prevent multiple conda processes from operating on an environment at the same time. I think it's necessary, and with changes in conda 4.4 it's also now technically possible to do correctly. Operating on an environment means you have write permissions to it, so locking the environment shouldn't be a problem. No so for the general case of package caches, which by design are allowed to be read-only. I don't know of any good/feasible technical way to lock a tree of files that are read-only. Other than having a separate, long-running agent process to coordinate the lock.

Yes, conda by default has two locations for both package caches and named environments.  Those associated with the root environment are the default when they are writable by the current user. The user workflow in Anaconda Platform as it's shaping up currently doesn't really have direct use of `conda` or `conda env` or `environment.yml` in it - nothing stops you from using them, but the beginning user docs would not need to go into them to get people started.

So we have the two commands that _are_ in the docs - at least the command line docs - `anaconda` and `anaconda-project`. The distinction between `anaconda` and `anaconda-project` is that `anaconda` affects the Anaconda platform server and `anaconda-project` affects your local project directory.

It's also possible to work on a notebook with only the GUI and no command line tools. In Jupyter there will be a "Packages" list and you can add packages to the list ... there's no manual environment manipulation. That work is currently on a branch at https://github.com/continuumio/bokeh-lab/tree/jlab19

The way we're doing this the workflow can be something like 1) click "new notebook", 2) see notebook now open, 3) click "add package" to add packages you need, 4) work on notebook, 5) click the save or share button to share with colleagues. The command line tools have equivalent operations. The "make environment", "activate environment", "install package", etc. steps are not done manually. There's also no need to manually edit `anaconda-project.yml` unless you feel like it.

This is an IDE-style experience, it won't be what everyone wants, but manual conda environment manipulation will always still be possible for those who choose to dig in.

`conda env attach` is a little bit of an orphan perhaps since it's too high-level for conda and too low-level/wrong-model for Anaconda Platform. So I don't know where it ends up. We don't have plans to do anything with it for Anaconda Platform afaik.

There's a lot of backstory on that miniconda link too.  We've debated killing `Miniconda-latest` off entirely, and even tried it once, but it just broke too many people.  Maybe it's time to try again though.

It has several problems.  First, it brings in python2, not python 3.  Just switching it to python3 would also be a surprise that will probably break people.  Additionally, while the primary purpose of Miniconda is to bootstrap *conda* onto a system, people also use it to get python.  So there's a bit of a conflation there.  It's very possible that at some point in the future, `Miniconda-latest` doesn't even give you a usable python in the base conda environment, and would solely be used for bootstrapping conda onto a system.

So, lot's of challenges there.  And we obviously still haven't figured out the best way to unwind it all yet.

> I think part of the problem here is that we need to define and document some sensible practices around the use of `track_features`.

For starters, we should create a list of configuration options that we want to address with `track_features`. So far, I've seen three: the compiler, the Python version, and the BLAS variant. Anything else?

> It seems problematic to me for some openblas packages to have a track_features value and for some not to.

Hopefully, this will only happen for historical reasons. Once the `track_features` mechanism is fully functional, `anaconda upload` should probably issue a warning or even an error if one attempts to upload a package without `track_features` specification when all (or most or any) existing variants do have such a specification (along with suitable options to override the default policy when necessary).

> It seems problematic to me for packages to have multiple, different `track_features` values. We're currently doing that with python but I'm encouraging us to move away from that for various reasons.

In case of Python, the obvious solution is to introduce compiler runtime packages that contain the appropriate `track_features` spec (and install the corresponding libraries) and have Python depend on one of those. Thus, Python has a `features` spec for the compiler, and a `track_features` spec for its own version. I guess that splitting up a package into a core and a dependency will solve the problem of multiple `track_features` values in other cases as well.

> So for example, what happens if you build a package with an openblas dependency and there are multiple track_features to choose from?

I'm not sure if I understand the question. Anyway, I imagine (and implemented) the following workflow: to build a package against specific dependencies, one first creates and activates a 'bootstrap' environment that contains precisely the desired dependency variants. Then, `conda build` (possibly with the help of jinja2 templates and/or command line options) recreates the bootstrap environment in the `_build` environment and adds missing dependencies (if any) according to the standard version resolution rules. Placing a dependency in the bootstrap environment thus ensures that conda won't accidentally pick an undesired variant. Additional machinery (like my `auto` proposal) then ensures that the newly built package precisely records the variants it was compiled against, so that `conda install` is guaranteed to create a consistent installation. Keyword arguments to `conda install` allow users to easily customize the variants to be installed.

Actually, @janschulz, I see no reason to deprecate them at all. It's clear from the updated `test_config.py` it was intentional to remove their support, but if people are using them, I just don't see why we shouldn't continue to do so. Booleans aren't hard to parse.

But here's a potentially important issue, @kalefranz, @msarahan. In `test_set_rc_string`, I see this comment:
```
    # We specifically test ssl_verify since it can be either a boolean or a string
``` 
Well, but that isn't really true, is it? After all, `ssl_verify` is in `rc_string_keys`, and the test was clearly changed to verify that `yes` is preserved as a string. So it's no longer true that `ssl_verify` is a boolean.

But that got me thinking: is _another_ part of the code expecting `ssl_verify` to be either a string or a boolean? The only place I see it is in `connection.py`, where it is used to initialize `self.verify` in the `CondaSession` object. The base `requests.Session` object uses this; see its docs [here](http://requests.readthedocs.io/en/master/api/?highlight=verify#requests.Session.verify); it _does_ expect it to be a boolean or a CA_BUNDLE path.

My point: I think `ssl_verify` _is_ intended to be interpreted as a YAML boolean whenever it is possible to do so. I've therefore modified the `CondaSession` constructor to use `yaml_bool` with a passthrough if it fails. Could this explain some of our stranger SSL errors?

Also, when using a wildcard with your regular expression, you need to encapsulate in single-quotes, and it's good practice to fully define your regex, whether with `^` and `$`, or use the `.*` notation to indicate a wildcard matching anything.  The following example will match indices beginning with (`^`) logstash-2015 and ending with anything else (`.*$`):

```
curator delete indices --regex '^logstash-2015.*$' --older-than 90 --time-unit days --timestring %Y.%m.%d
```

Okay, there's something in capitalization in resources. If I just go with straight all-lowercase names, this works (I appended my initials to the vault name to make it unique):

```
az keyvault create --resource-group resourcegroupname --name vaultnameikf --enabled-for-deployment

az keyvault certificate create --vault-name vaultnameikf -n cert1 \
   -p “$(az keyvault certificate get-default-policy)”

secrets=$(az keyvault secret list-versions --vault-name vaultnameikf \
   -n cert1 --query “[?attributes.enabled].id” -o tsv)

vm_secrets=$(az vm format-secret -s “$secrets”)
```

By twist of fate, we have naming conventions in our docs that typically uses camel-casing. As such, the following fails with the error initially noted:

```
az keyvault create --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myVaultIKF --enabled-for-deployment

secrets=$(az keyvault secret list-versions --vault-name myVaultIKF \
   -n cert1 --query “[?attributes.enabled].id” -o tsv)

vm_secrets=$(az vm format-secret -s “$secrets”)
```

Hi @rambo-msft 
Does that mean this is out of date? http://azure-storage.readthedocs.io/en/latest/blob.html

Some ideas on how to deal with this bug.

1. We could schedule `abort_pool()` to run, oh, 6 hours after the program starts. Either by sending a SIGUSR1 from the EC2 shell script that invokes the job or adding a master timeout right in the [main job monitor loop](https://github.com/openaddresses/machine/blob/17da30729e154a5f28d34a3e7e97c7ffeff3774b/openaddr/jobs.py#L112). 
2. I'm doing a run again with [maxchildtasks](https://github.com/openaddresses/machine/blob/17da30729e154a5f28d34a3e7e97c7ffeff3774b/openaddr/jobs.py#L103)=None, the default for multiprocessing. This means Python reuses the worker process instead of starting a new one. I prefer the idea of starting new processes but maybe that's exactly what is triggering a bug in the pool code.
3. I'm going to read the `multiprocessing.Pool()` code closely and try to understand where the problem may come from. I've also set it up so I can get to `pdb` in the program to try to debug state. I don't have a lot of hope for figuring this out.
4. We could replace `multiprocessing.Pool` with our own multiprocessing-based job manager. I think about 90% of the current code would stay the same, just need new code for forking processes. I hate to do this, but if the problem is a bug in `Pool` that'd be one way to avoid it.

The test function is synchronous so I suppose the tests are run synchronously. The thing I'm testing spawns a process, wait for it and assert. There is only one thing done at a time.

If I want to make it asynchronous it means I will have to move the test to a coroutine and the test itself will do a `run_until_complete` on that coroutine.

There is no point of doing so because the event loop will be busy with only one task. And the perk of using `asyncio.create_subprocess` is also not so good but it is actually using subprocess behind the scene (it's just a wrapper).

So unless you know how to make the tests run asynchronously in parallel like mocha.parallel, there is no real advantage to this.

`requests` and `aiohttp` are commonly used by other libraries - changing those are out of scope for many users and use-cases 

To explain further
 `requests`  **does not use** the system key store, they can be pointed at  CA file via ENV to work around this.
This then also updates where SSL is looking, printing out `ssl.get_default_verify_paths()` shows this.

aiohttp **does use** the system key store, via SSL. 
But if an application has `requests` calls this has changed where SSL looks for ca certs. 
and seemingly aiohttp does not follow where SSL is looking 

We're left where an application can make no obvious use of requests and aiohttp and a companies own root_ca certificate.

> E.g. 'templates' could be an additional section of the 'New' menu in the notebook interface - but this only works if there aren't too many.

When I think of my use-case for notebook templates, I think of them as essentially _kernels that come with some notebook data pre-populated_. This concept lends itself to including templates in the "New" menu.

Perhaps some heavy users will have so many kernels and templates that the New menu becomes unwieldy, but my intuition at this point says that this should be the exception, not the rule. 

We also need to consider how to create templates. At first pass, it is probably fine to require the user (or package installer) to copy an .ipynb to the templates folder. Eventually, it may be nice to allow users to "Save as template", making it easy for someone to save the boiler-plate notebook code unique to their workflow.

It probably comes from the fact that the convolution call [bottoms out in a call to `np.einsum`](https://github.com/HIPS/autograd/blob/7cc6b12be84533a8ee9e9ffa90756851a483d226/autograd/scipy/signal.py#L51), which IIRC is single-threaded.

It's unfortunate that numpy and scipy don't have a good neural net-style convolution function that autograd can wrap. We could write our own in terms of a call to `np.dot` (and some data striding, and some zero padding for the VJP function when using strided filters) and that would at least be multi-threaded.

@the-gigi I think @mreiferson and I understand where you are coming from, and I appreciate your time in sharing your thoughts here.

On a tangential note, i'm curious the hear if you use the built in backoff? It's typical (and intentional) that backoff slows processing to a trickle when you have a transient failure, and in turn that also means you very slowly work through your retry count.

I think there are probably two tangible changes we can make here:

1) increase the default max retry. While I think there should continue to be *a* default limit, I'd be happy with something < 25.

2) update the default implementation of `giving_up()` to archive the request body. This is what i do in my use of `pynsq` and i can't think of a good reason we shouldn't have that as the default implementation. It could write to CWD by default, and we could add a new argument to control that location.

@tgoodlet if i write an impl like ``def myhook(arg1, arg2=20)`` i kind of expect that when someone calls ``myhook(arg1=10)`` to see ``arg2=20`` passed in. Now we have a spec as well that might have a default value and according @RonnyPfannschmidt and @nicoddemus 's view would override my impl one.  So far the spec is used for validation only, to keep callers and impls correct signature-wise and i think we should try keep it that way.  Allowing ``=None`` for spec arguments is what we minimally need to prevent calling sites to break when we add an argument.  If we also move towards saying impls can only ever use None as well, then there is never a question of which default is used at an impl side. 

IOW, i suggest we consider only allowing None default values for specs and maybe also for impls.  Or find some other way to cater the case of wanting to have callers with different nums of arguments work.

https://github.com/gaborbernat/tox3/blob/master/tests/conftest.py#L20

```python
@pytest.yield_fixture()
def event_loop():
    """pytest-asyncio customization"""
    if sys.platform != "win32":
        asyncio.set_event_loop(None)  # see https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest-asyncio/issues/73
    loop = get_event_loop()
    if sys.platform != "win32":
        # on UNIX we also need to attach the loop to the child watcher for asyncio.subprocess
        policy = asyncio.get_event_loop_policy()
        watcher = asyncio.SafeChildWatcher()
        watcher.attach_loop(loop)
        policy.set_child_watcher(watcher)
    try:
        yield loop
    finally:
        loop.close()
```

Setting the event loop to None can be removed if we check that we only re-attach if the loop is not closed yet in the finalizer. Or so I think.

Starting with a connection on :80 is a limit to the http-01 challenge type that webroot uses, and it exists for [security reasons](https://www.ietf.org/mail-archive/web/acme/current/msg00524.html).  If you really have inbound 80 blocked, and can't automatically stop and start nginx immediately before and after renewal, your next best bet might be to renew with the nginx plugin.  But please be aware that it is experimental and buggy.

If you want to try that, back up your configuration first, and expect to encounter bugs.  Then follow the [developer instructions](https://letsencrypt.readthedocs.org/en/latest/contributing.html#running-a-local-copy-of-the-client) to get a copy of the client with the nginx plugin available.  You can activate the venv or just run `/path/to/letsencrypt/venv/bin/letsencrypt certonly --nginx --renew-by-default -d example.com,www.example.com,www.etc.com`.  You should then be able to reload nginx to pick up the new cert that will be deployed in `/etc/letsencrypt/live`.

We have two options here:

1. Release a 0.5.0 version of `certbot` containing shim packages around `letsencrypt`. This is a fair amount of extra work to create temporary shim packages so `letsencrypt-auto` continues to work and pass tests until the 0.6.0 release.
2. Wait to do this until the 0.7.0 release. Have `certbot-auto/letsencrypt-auto` use the `letsencrypt` shim packages during 0.6.0. This means `*-auto` has to download two extra packages (`letsencrypt` and `letsencrypt-apache`) and we'll have to do an `0.7.0` release of the shim packages to allow `*-auto` to update to the new version of itself that points to the `certbot` packages.

I'm in favor of option 2. Either approach involves doing another release of shim packages and we already have the `letsencrypt` shim packages. Additionally, there is still plenty of work to be done for 0.6.0 and there is less of a deadline for 0.7.0

I would argue that being able to specify an existing key is really important for supporting HPKP, so that users can set up a backup key that is stored offline.

Browsers will not enforce HPKP unless the header specifies the hash for two or more more unrelated keys. More specifically, browsers will only use and cache the HPKP header if there is at least one hash that matches a certificate in the certificate chain for the site, as well as at least one hash that is not in the certificate chain.

It is possible to use an intermediate CA (or a root CA) instead of the leaf node certificate with HPKP, but then users would have to pick a different intermediate certificate or CA as their backup (or use a backup key as the second pin). This would be easier to use with how letsencrypt currently works, but it removes the flexibility of choosing the most specific certificate in order to minimize the attack surface that HPKP mitigates. Using intermediate certificates as pins is significantly more secure than no HPKP, but if any of the 2+ pinned intermediate certificates is used to create a fraudulent certificate then an HPKP-protected site would be out of luck. It also means deciding in advance which other CA an operator wants to do business with, and that decision cannot be changed if the Let's Encrypt intermediate CA is compromised and the operator is forced to re-issue their certificate with the other CA (after which they have to add the new Let's Encrypt intermediate certificate to the header and then they have to wait the HPKP max-age before they can start using such a certificate again, assuming they still want to use Let's Encrypt).

This is a great discussion and I am very excited about the broad agreement on the design and usefulness of this proposal; thanks all!

Here are some specific thoughts from me:

1. Where to put the name of the tool dependency?
 In CWL we only execute a single tool within each tool description. Therefore in the interests of "Don't Repeat Yourself" I think extracting the tool name from the `baseCommand` is the kindest choice to make for those writing CWL descriptions. For the case of naming conflicts we can set the `id` to an [IRI](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Internationalized_Resource_Identifier) to disambiguate. This could be the [bio.tools](https://bio.tools) unique identifier, a Debian identifer, or any other namespace.

 Example: [snap](http://snap.cs.berkeley.edu/), in Debian we had to rename it to [snap-aligner](https://tracker.debian.org/pkg/snap-aligner) due to name conflicts which makes it a good candidate for explicit identification.

 ``` yaml
cwlVersion: cwl:draft-3
class: CommandLineTool
id: http://packages.qa.debian.org/snap-aligner
baseCommand: [snap-aligner]
inputs: []
outputs: []
```

2. Wait, what about tools with co-dependencies? Fair enough, some programs run differently or have expanded functionality when other programs or libraries are co-installed.

 In this instance only I agree that additional information in the CWL tool description is warranted.

 This should be stronger than a hint, so I propose that we add an optional field `with` to `CommandLineTool` that contains an array of IRIs to resources that enhance the program specified by the `baseCommand` or `id` fields.

 Example: The Mira assembler has an optional 12s rRNA database that enhances its ability to detect rRNA & rDNA. While the name `mira` is fairly unique, we specify an ID out of an abundance of caution.

 ```
cwlVersion: cwl:draft-3
class: CommandLineTool
id: http://packages.qa.debian.org/mira
with: http://packages.debian.org/mira-rfam-12s-rrna
baseCommand: [mira]
inputs: []
outputs: []
```

 The direct dependencies of a tool, like the Python `screed` library for k-h-mer, have no business being listed in the tool description. That information is already known to packaging systems.

3. What about versions?

 Versioning is hard, there is not an agreed upon way to evaluate version numbers, despite semver.org. 

 For example, Debian has a nice standard for comparing version number but if upstream does their own thing Debian maintainers rewrite upstream's numbers to Debian's standard with an embedded copy of the original version. Obviously other packaging systems take different approaches.

 Example: The current version of `snap` is `1.0beta.18`. Debian's version is `0.18~1.0~beta.18+dfsg1-3`: this sorts before `1.0`, contains all the components of the original version, and notes that Debian modified the source tarball once (`+dfsg1`) and this is the third release of this particular version in Debian (`-3`).

 This is too Debian specific to include in the CWL standard.

 For runtime control, the [job input object requirements](https://github.com/common-workflow-language/common-workflow-language/issues/210) should be expanded to specify required version numbers or other platform specific hints.

 Example job input document syntax:
 ``` yaml
cwl:requirements:
  - ToolVersionRequirements: { https://bio.tools/tool/ucdavis.edu/khmer: 2.0 }
```

 For repeatability purposes the output of the execution on a CWL tool or CWL workflow should contain the exact versions run as part of provenance reporting. This will be standardized post-1.0 as part of the [RO](http://www.researchobject.org/)+CWL bundle project. In the meantime, implementors are encouraged and allowed to add any metadata using any vocabulary to the output object.

```text
                                837
```

Supposing you sequenced this gene of a person and aligned his sequence to NM_001145026.1 and found the variant at 837T>A. Then if you align his sequence to genomic NC_000012.11, what variant will you get? It's `NC_000012.11:g.80860626_80860627insCATAACTTTTTG`, right?

The fundamental logic behind variant mapping is that the original variant and the mapped variant should refer to the same altered sequence. Only in this way the two variants are equal. The altered sequence constructed from `NM_001145026.1:c.837T>A` at transcript level and that constructed from `NC_000012.11:g.80860626_80860627insCATAACTTTTTG` at genomic level is the same. No other types of mapped variants could derive the same altered sequence. Thus, I think `NC_000012.11:g.80860626_80860627insCATAACTTTTTG` is the only reasonable mapped variant for `NM_001145026.1:c.837T>A` in this transcript-genome alignment gap.

These examples demonstrate the hgvs package considers alignment gaps and gives the correct mapped variants, which is an important distinct feature compared to other tools.

In practice I've found that YOLO9000's accuracy isn't as good as SSD or Faster R-CNN (speed is another story, YOLO is very speedy). YOLO can also be more challenging to train, depending on the task. As you also noted, YOLO doesn't work well with small objects. 

It's impossible to say "this architecture is always the best" because (as we know) which architecture + training procedure is best is _highly_ dependent on the dataset, task, and project.

My general rule of thumb is that if speed isn't a concern, Faster R-CNN is a good starting point. If speed is paramount and accuracy can suffer a bit, give YOLO a try. If a middle ground is needed, start with SSD variants (SSD + MobileNet for speed; SSD + Inception for potentially higher accuracy but longer inference time) and go from there.

Again, that is just my rule of thumb from my own personal experience training these networks on a number of datasets. Whether or not it holds up to a particular task is highly dependent on the data and overall goals.

```
(set-logic HORN)
(declare-rel $END_QUERY ())
(declare-rel INV_REL$0
 ((Array Int Int) Int Int Int Int (Array Int Int) Int Int Int Int))
(declare-var a0 (Array Int Int))
(declare-var a1 (Array Int Int))
(declare-var i0 Int)
(declare-var i1 Int)
(declare-var r0 Int)
(declare-var r1 Int)
(declare-var t0 Int)
(declare-var t1 Int)
(declare-var x0 Int)
(declare-var x1 Int)
(rule
 (=>
  (and
   (and (and (< i0 1000) (<= x0 (select a0 i0)))
    (not (and (< i1 1000) (<= x1 (select a1 i1)))))
   (INV_REL$0 a0 x0 r0 i0 t0 a1 x1 r1 i1 t1))
  $END_QUERY))
(rule
 (=>
  (and
   (and (not (and (< i0 1000) (<= x0 (select a0 i0))))
    (and (< i1 1000) (<= x1 (select a1 i1))))
   (INV_REL$0 a0 x0 r0 i0 t0 a1 x1 r1 i1 t1))
  $END_QUERY))
(rule
 (=> (and (= a0 a1) (= x0 x1))
  (INV_REL$0 a0 x0 r0 0 0 a1 x1 r1 0 0)))
(rule
 (=>
  (and
   (< i0 1000) (<= x0 (select a0 i0))
   (< i1 1000) (<= x1 (select a1 i1))
   (INV_REL$0 a0 x0 r0 i0 t0 a1 x1 r1 i1 t1))
  (INV_REL$0 a0 x0 r0 (+ i0 1) t0 a1 x1 r1 (+ i1 1) t1)))
(rule
 (let ((t0$0 0))
```

I'm afraid this is one of those cases that looks simple but isn't easy for the solver. Z3 translates this into bit-vectors, then bit-blasts, then runs a SAT solver and almost all the time is spent in the SAT solver. Implicitly, it enumerates lots of different cases before it finishes. That strategy is good for finding counter-examples, but it will always be inefficient for finding proofs of unsatisfiability over large bit-width floats.

We have techniques to handle small cores in big problems and for problems that use large floating-point numbers when they need only small ones, but neither of them applies here. In general, proving non-trivial things about complicated sets of floating-point numbers, will always be slow, except if the bit-width is very small, or we provide the right lemmas to Z3 so that it can cut some proofs short. 

For this particular example, it's fairly easy to check that the solver essentially enumerates. For instance, if we replace one/minus_one like so:

    (define-const one Float32 (fp #b0 #x00 #b00000000000000000000001))
    (define-const minus_one Float32 (fp #b1 #x00 #b00000000000000000000001))

(i.e., to the smallest non-zero float), then the solver takes only a couple seconds. And, as we move the '1' bit further to the left, it takes more and more time. 

I think that to a human this problem looks 'easy' because we implicitly proof it by induction (e.g., +1 means it will also hold for +2), but a SAT solver isn't able to do that. In general we might require quantified lemmas/axioms to do that efficiently, but that would take us into a whole different set of challenges.

If it would have been possible (but I don't think it currently is in Lasagne), you should also save the internal state of the optimizer so you can restore that as well when you continue the training, as it contains a lot of information that is of value for the training. If you use the `nesterov_momentum` optimizer for example, you should ideally also save the `velocity` vector in the function `apply_nesterov_momentum` so you can continue the training without having to reset the velocity to zero when you start training again.

Resetting the velocity to zero means that you loose "kinetic energy", which if you are unlucky may make you get stuck in a local minimum for which you need a certain amount of "energy" in order to escape.

Bonus advantage: Things like `ReshapeLayer` will fail on construction rather than on the first `get_output_shape_for()` call triggered by creating a layer on top of them.
Bonus disadvantage: Every attribute required for `get_output_shape_for()` to work has to be set before calling the super constructor, because this will call `get_output_shape_for(self.input_shape)`. If setting up the attributes already requires `self.input_shape` to be present, we're out of luck. (Haven't checked whether this is the case anywhere.)

If we make it a property instead, we lose the bonus advantage and the bonus disadvantage. I'm undecided. The attribute would be nice, but it's a bit weird to not have the super constructor call be the first statement.

My thinking there is that it could be implemented in a fairly straightforward manner, without many changes. On reflection, an overloaded Walker may be more trouble than its worth.

Here's the current main loop in `copy_dir`. 

```python
    with manage_fs(src_fs, writeable=False) as src_fs:
        with manage_fs(dst_fs, create=True) as dst_fs:
            with src_fs.lock(), dst_fs.lock():
                dst_fs.makedir(_dst_path, recreate=True)
                for dir_path, dirs, files in walker.walk(src_fs, _src_path):
                    copy_path = combine(
                        _dst_path,
                        frombase(_src_path, dir_path)
                    )
                    for info in dirs:
                        dst_fs.makedir(
                            info.make_path(copy_path),
                            recreate=True
                        )
                    for info in files:
                        copy_file(
                            src_fs,
                            info.make_path(dir_path),
                            dst_fs,
                            info.make_path(copy_path)
                        )
```

If you changed the `walker.walk` to `walker.info` you have an info object for the modification times of the source filesystem. You could then call `scandir` on the destination filesystem to get info for the destination.

Then its just a matter of adding a condition around the `copy_file` method.

This has the advantage that you can get the info for the directories on the src and dst in one call, which is quite efficient. It will be much faster for FTP or network filesystems.

```python
    class RunnerImport(ConsoleClientPlaceMixin, View):
        model = Runner
        from_encoding = "utf-8"

            else:
                return self.resource_class
    
        def get_import_resource_class(self):
            """
            Returns ResourceClass to use for import.
            """
            return self.get_resource_class()

        def post(self, request, *args, **kwargs):
            """
            Perform a dry_run of the import to make sure the import will not
            result in errors.  If no error, save the user
            uploaded file to a local temp file that will be used by
            'process_import' for the actual import.
            """
            errors = []
            resource = self.get_import_resource_class()()
            try:
                fileName, ext = os.path.splitext(
                    self.request.FILES['import_file'].name
                )
                fileExtension = ext.replace('.', '').lower()
            except MultiValueDictKeyError:
                errors.append(_("Please select a file"))
    
            context = self.get_context_data(**kwargs)
    
            form = ManagersImportToClientForm(
                request.POST or None,
                request.FILES or None,
            )
    
            if form.is_valid():
                input_format = self.formats[fileExtension]()
                import_file = form.cleaned_data['import_file']
                # first always write the uploaded file to disk as it may be a
                # memory file or else based on settings upload handlers
                with tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(
                        delete=False, dir=settings.TEMP_DIR) as uploaded_file:
                    for chunk in import_file.chunks():
                        uploaded_file.write(chunk)
    
                # then read the file, using the proper format-specific mode
                with open(uploaded_file.name, 'rU') as uploaded_import_file:
                    # warning, big files may exceed memory
                    data = uploaded_import_file.read()
                    if not input_format.is_binary() and self.from_encoding:
                        data = force_text(data, self.from_encoding)
                    dataset = input_format.create_dataset(data)
    
                    result = resource.import_data(dataset, dry_run=True,
                                                  raise_errors=True)
                context['result'] = result
```

OKAY HERE'S THE PROBLEM

On intel processors, the memory that will eventually be relocated to a pointer into libc or whatever contains a pointer to immediately after that function's PLT stub. This is so that when the dynamic loader uses lazy resolution, an attempt to call the library function by jumping to that pointer (from the plt stub, presumably) will instead jump to the stub-suffix, which is a small snippet of code that jumps back into the dynamic loader in order to perform the resolution lazily.

On ARM, all the pointers point to the same resolution stub. This means that the hack that we use to find the intel plt stubs just straight up doesn't work on ARM, since the value in the GOT no longer has anything to do with the function that slot is for.

The main problem is that the only thing that makes a PLT stub special is that it loads the value out of the GOT, which is something that's hard to find in the general case without a lot of analysis. Even what we have right now for intel stuff is a hack that is more-or-less compiler specific and will fail in a lot of cases.

That being said, there are probably some pretty good heuristics for figuring out what the ARM stubs are, too. I'll look into that.

- I told you the interpretation backwards. Sorry about that. `0#24` is a bitvector of value 0 and length 24.
- The 0x prefix just signifies if the value is printed in hex or not. The choice of whether to print in hex or not is [based on the length of the bitvector](https://github.com/angr/claripy/blob/master/claripy/ast/base.py#L463).

Constraints are only added on conditional branches. This is a pretty basic element of symbolic execution.

The reason you don't see your variable in the tracking is because the tracking only works with variables that were registered with it. angr does this automatically with any variables it creates, but for variables you create you need to register it manually. You can do this by either creating the variable through the `state.solver.BVS()` constructor and providing a `key=` argument as a tuple of hierarchical descriptors for the variable, or using `state.solver.register_variable(ast, key)`.

However it looks like that's not what you want.

In this case if you want to track the correspondence between the input and the output you should just look directly at the output. Since it's going to stdout you want to look directly at stdout - `state.posix.stdout` to be precise. This is a SimFile - a complicated interface for managing file streams. IIRC the default stdout is actually a `SimPackets` to be able to better handle symbolic-length writes, so you want to look at `state.posix.stdout.content`, which is a list of tuples of `(content, content-length)`. Note that this only applies if you're running angr installed from github - SimFiles were refactored last month and changed a lot, and the pypi version hasn't caught up.

My fix is wrong. Ignore it.

In your original example, the input variable `x` of function `test` is `0x1000`, which should be an uninitialized memory cell (I didn't see you initializing it anywhere in your script). That means `*x` is fully unconstrained (can be any value), and `&a[*x - 10]` could point anywhere in the memory. If `&a[*x - 10]` points to an uninitialized memory cell, then `a[*x - 10]` is again a fully unconstrained symbolic value, which will be constrained to different values (like `0`, `1`, `2`, `3`) based on different paths that angr executes afterwards. However, `a[*x - 10] == 0` does not necessarily mean `*x == 10`, since it still holds when `&a[*x - 10]` points to an unconstrained memory cell. Therefore angr is giving you the expected result.

However, angr should warn the user when some "symbolic code" is evaluated: right now, when some symbolic bytes are executed, it will just call the solver, concretize them (considering the first _random_ solution obtained by Z3, see `_bytes_from_state()` in `lifter.py`) and then lift these concrete bytes to VEX. Although this strategy is very reasonable, I think that angr could, at least, evaluate if more than one solution is possible for the symbolic bytes and warn the user about this fact. To deal with some "easy" cases, it could even evaluate if the number[*] of possible solutions are less than a threshold and in this case fork the state for each possible solution. It is likely that several of these forked states will probably execute some illegal code and thus die very soon. This strategy is similar to what angr does when a symbolic read is performed. The main difference is that you can't use ite expression, but you are forced to fork.

@alexpreynolds also want to mention that, in mygene.info, these are two pairs of related fields:

* **symbol**:  official gene symbol
* **alias**:   other gene symbols in-use

and 

* **name**: office gene name
* **other_names**: other gene names in-use

MyGene.info always keeps the values of these fields in-sync with NCBI and Ensembl.

Depending what users' input terms are, and what they want to return (strictly official symbol/name matches or loosely matching including aliases and other names), they can adjust what fields should be passed to the "scopes" parameter in querymany.

> the git SHA1 short code that is returned by the gitpython library

you sure that is a `unicode` string and not a `bytes` string? Use `tounicode()` function to ensure it is a `unicode` string (import it from `fonttools.misc.py23` module).

Concatenating unicode and bytes in Python 2 would automatically decode the latter (upgrade them to unicode) using the default ascii encoding. In Python 3, attempting to do `u"abc" + b"abc"` raises TypeError (rightly so). So, in a py2.py3 world, it's better you make sure you are dealing with either or the other, and not mix them up.

The NameRecord `string` attribute can take either a unicode string or a bytes string. In the first case, it will encode it automatically for you next time you compile; in the latter case, it will assume you already encoded the bytes string with the right encoding and will just write it as is. If the bytes were not encoded with the right encoding, you may get garbage like the one in the screenshot.

What worked for me is to add this to the callback parsing function:

    def parse_item(self, response):
        """Parse response into item also create new requests."""
 
        page = RescrapItem()
        ...
        yield page

        if isinstance(response, (HtmlResponse, SplashTextResponse)):
            seen = set()
            for n, rule in enumerate(self._rules):
                links = [lnk for lnk in rule.link_extractor.extract_links(response)
                         if lnk not in seen]
                if links and rule.process_links:
                    links = rule.process_links(links)
                for link in links:
                    seen.add(link)
                    r = SplashRequest(url=link.url, callback=self._response_downloaded, 
                                                  args=SPLASH_RENDER_ARGS)
                    r.meta.update(rule=rule, link_text=link.text)
                    yield rule.process_request(r)

Hi @saurabh02 

I think it's a good question. In my book, there are a few reasons to go with optional dependency:

- the disk size of the dependency is large
- the dependency is difficult to install, e.g., cannot be auto-installed
- the dependency has some chance of breaking the auto-install (i.e., someone who wants to ```pip install matminer``` gets an error during the installation of the dependency - so they can't install matminer automatically, even if they never planned on using that sub-feature).
- The dependency has other dependencies that have one of the above characteristics.

Based on those criteria, I don't see a reason to make citrination-client optional and we can keep it as required so the user has access to the functionality without extra effort. If we find that the dependency breaks one of those criteria in the future, we should make it optional.

We can keep plotly for the same reasons. Again, if it starts breaking things we'll make it optional.

You can separate RL algorithms into on-policy and off-policy. Sometimes it is usefull to process a bunch of data before updating, this allows you for example to prepare a batch and use gpu resources better or to train in multiple threads/gpus/machines. Off-policy algorithms process "off-policy" samples, that is, data that has not been generated with your current policy.

The reason this is happening is so that gradients don't flow into the target Q function. Since 2 networks are being used here if you allow gradients to update the targetQ this becomes improper. This stop_gradient allows you to essentially treat targetQ as a constant that is the target update.

    of source code and graph tracebacks. I think it's a more reliable solution than disabling source code /
    traceback display on the tensorboard side. It should help address cases in which source code
    confidentiality and/or the network communication overhead associated with the code and traceback
    data is of concern.

2. Clicking the blue underlined links in the line-by-line node list in the source code will do the
     following:
     * Focus on the corresponding node in the Node List (top left quadrant)
     * Focus on the corresponding node in the Graph Visualizer (top right quadrant)
     * Highlight the corresponding tensor in the Tensor Value Overview (bottom right quadrant)
    Clicking the blue underlined links in the full stack sticky dialog will scroll the source code to the 
    corresponding line, and switch to a different source code file first if necessary.

Currently the full content of a source code file is displayed. I think it's simpler than displaying a small
number of lines at a time. The ability to inspect any part of a file is nice. The network overhead should
be acceptable, given that most user files are reasonably sized (<1000 lines, or about 8 kB). Note that we
currently omit files that belong to the TF python library itself (i.e., see the grayed out items in the
full stack sticky dialog).

Hmm - I see that this was moved, but never *removed* from openthread...

I guess there are two issues then:

a) Just how stable is the SPINEL protocol? How often do we expect new packets to be added to the protocol, and how do we coordinate updating *both* SPINEL and Openthread at the same time.  I am concerned about the corner case of requiring an update to *both* pyspinel and openthread in order to get an acceptance test to pass.

b) The other issue is a test/build environment issue and the way the environment works.

When using Travis - it always gives you a "clean machine" and an "isolated machine".

In contrast, other test platforms (ie: Jenkins) generally give you a shared user - with separate build directories. 

To solve the above problem (the specific version problem) - we could move the "pip install" from the travis setup to the Makefiles that use them.

And install a specific/specified version into the ${openthread}/build directory, instead of:  ```pip install --user```, use  ```pip install --target ${openthread}/build``` then set the PYTHONPATH variable for the tests that require the package. All of that could be done in the test specific top level Makefile

Another problem that a semi-shared environment like Jenkins has is this:  Often Jenkins kicks off multiple jobs - and that means you can easily have two tests running at the same time.  If each job installs its own tools, and they install to a SHARED location  JOB (A) may reinstall the shared tool that JOB(B) is currently using... 

Again - I think installing into the local build directory solves that.

A call to `otPlatRadioTransmitDone()` should normally result in the following call path:

1. `Mac::TransmitDoneTask()`
2. `Mac::SentFrame()`
3. `Mac::ScheduleNextTransmission()`
4. `Mac::NextOperation()`
5. `otPlatRadioReceive()`

On return from this path, the receive channel should be set back to the `Mac::mChannel` value.

That said, one issue I do see is that on a Channel Access Failure, Abort, or No Ack error, `Mac::SentFrame()` will not call `Mac::ScheduleNextTransmission()` (and thus never call `otPlatRadioReceive()`.  Do you think this is the issue you are running into?

The main obstacle is that numpy's sparse storage is fundamentally different than Eigen's, so it isn't possible to have one simply reference the other's data.  For dense types, all you need is a chunk of data and strides to map between them.  That isn't to say something better can't be done, just that no one has looked into it.

The overhead is going to be the effort involved in copying it, which will typically come down to the number of non-sparse values that need to be copied from one side to a new type on the other side.

In the meantime, you could try another Let's Encrypt client.

https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/list-of-client-implementations/2103

There are several native Windows clients, and there are also some lighter-weight Unix clients that have fewer dependencies, and hence might have a higher likelihood of running successfully within the Ubuntu layer for Windows.

You seem to be on a release before version 0.12.0. The config options 'iterations' and 'interval' for clf.connect() were added in version 0.12 but existed before for the low level clf.sense() method. Since version 0.12 the relevant code section is:
```python
def _rdwr_connect(self, options, terminate):
    target = self.sense(*options['targets'],
                        iterations=options['iterations'],
                        interval=options['interval'])
```

I expect that DDD writer (altogether with SPC writer) may not necessary be distributed as pip package, but can be used by experienced user by cloning GIT repository and running files as python scripts.

Both existing DDD and future SPC do not depend on pytrip98 code at all and are independent on pytrip releasing. They generate files which can be directly used by TRiP98 without need of pytrip itself.
Downloading whole https://github.com/pytrip/pytrip to get such a code doesn't make sense (in GIT you cannot clone subdirectory of a repo).

Most of libraries are distributed in the form of many repositories, see: https://github.com/gitlabhq, https://github.com/scipy, https://github.com/numpy.

In order to avoid user seeing too scattered system we could have a chapter in pytrip documentation describing how to make DDD+SPC files (or redirect to pytrip/ddd repo to learn that).

With CUBE data writer it is different - it needs to use pytrip DosCube class to save the data. Thus it is better to be for it a part of pytrip library (i.e. utils). In this form it will be more like "SH12A data importer".

@alykhantejani @daavoo Yes, for the masks there's not much to be done, since they are, in essence additional channels on the image. The functions for applying these transformations to bounding boxes will, however, need to be of a different flavor.

Tensorflow's object detection (AFAIR) code uses a separate classes for boxes and points and so on with transforms defined on them individually, but one only needs to support transformation on arrays of normalized co-ordinates to deal with most of these tasks (including convex masks and so on).

I'd personally prefer to extend relevant transformations in ```functional.py``` by having them take on additional ```2d-coordinates``` parameter that get co-transformed when given. Having separate functions for feature inputs and one for co-ordinate inputs is not going to be pretty IMO.

@killent I have a number of bounding box-aware resize/crop/pad... functions implemented. I'm currently using the cython nms function from,
https://github.com/rbgirshick/py-faster-rcnn 
and am in the process of porting ```sample_distorted_bounding_box``` from Tensorflow, 
https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/master/tensorflow/core/kernels/sample_distorted_bounding_box_op.cc

These are really the only non-pythonic bits necessary for fast inference/training on single-shot detectors (SSD, YOLO) (the addition of these ops in tensorflow coincides with their object detection efforts). Faster RCNN AFAIK only requires ROI-pooling in-addition, which is just strided slicing (or resampling..); all the anchor-box infrastructure is fairly similar (atleast from what I remember). 

Tensorflow has a (approximate) bi-partite matching matcher using the Hungarian algorithm, but this doesn't seem to be widely used. I'm not sure if there was a C++ kernel for this (probably not). The more common hard-mining functions can be written with vectorizable code and nms, though.

Now that I think about it, I wonder if some these functions (nms) should go into pytorch.

Interesting, so each worker actually has two threads.

1. The "main thread" executes an infinite loop in which it blocks while waiting for a new task from the local scheduler. When it gets the task, it executes it. Then it issues another blocking request to the local scheduler for a new task, and this repeats.

2. The "import thread", which in the background waits for notifications about new remote function definitions, fetches the pickled remote functions from Redis, and unpickles them.

Unpickling a remote function (using cloudpickle in this case) will cause the relevant modules to be imported, and presumably some initialization is happening there on the "import thread". And then the stuff is getting used from the "main thread" which is causing problems. Does that sound like what's happening to you?

FYI, it turns out a lot of this work is something `Numba` has wrestled with.

The reason is that they are solving a *very* similar problem: instead of pickling a function, they compile it to LLVM IR, so they have to statically capture the closure of the function too, do full-on type-inference/type-checking, and and save the results in a binary format, etc. The main conceptual difference is their process is practically irreversible (you can't decompile LLVM IR) but aside from that, _very_ much of what they do is surprisingly similar.

I don't know if it's possible to outright use Numba's code, but I would at least follow their footsteps as much as possible, whether terms of what to support vs. not support, or whether in terms of what the API should look like, or whether in terms of what solutions to apply to problems that come up with respect to pickling, or anything else. Numba is already far more restrictive in terms of what code is allowed so I think Ray isn't doing too badly haha.

For Python2.7, Python3.3 and Python3.4 there are wheels on PyPi that include the compiled binaries for `python-librtmp`, so I think it would work (I have no way to test it) if you could use Python 2.7, 3.3 or 3.4. If not, you gotta compile it.

Hi there,

Thank you for the interest and report.

Confirmed - this occurs when there is unread output and timeout is used.

As a work-around, read buffers prior to calling `join`. Consuming buffers is required for the join with timeout case and will be enforced as an API change in next release.

From further testing, there are cases where a join with a timeout will return immediately while commands are still running. These seem to be unavoidable as timeout is passed on to the `select` call and is applied to the socket while the socket could be running multiple commands. On one call to `join` the socket can be finished prior to timeout, while on the next call another remote command will have started which is not finished. Hence the attempt to use `eof` as an indicator of completion.

However, as that fails when buffers have not been read, and reading buffers can be subject to same timeout, a `join` with a timeout will have to force consumption of buffers.

Eg, consider this code:

```
client.join(client.run_command('echo blah; sleep 15'), timeout=16)
```

On the call to `join`, the first command will have finished already and join returns immediately as nothing is blocking the socket where the timeout is applied. Second command may or may not have started (race condition) but there is no indication from server that a command has started, only that it has been accepted. A subsequent join call will wait ~15sec for the second command as the socket is blocked for the duration of that command.

However, after the first join call not all commands have finished which is misleading.

This code, on the other hand:

```
output = client.run_command('echo blah; sleep 15', timeout=16)
for host, host_out in output.items():
    for line in host_out.stdout:
        pass
    for line in host_out.stderr:
        pass
````

Ensures all commands have finished as buffers are read and `eof` is reached.

The description above is a bit confusing... for example, it links to some complicated code using ctypes, but that's only needed for old python versions we don't support anyway. For us it can be fairly simple. Basically we need something like a function that takes the family/type/proto that the user gave us, and fixes them up as best we can (which might be more or less, depending on the OS). For example (untested):

```python
def _fix_attrs_for_fileno(family, type, proto, fileno):
    # Wrap the raw fileno into a socket object
    sockobj = _stdlib_socket.socket(fileno=fileno)
    try:
        if hasattr(socket, "SO_DOMAIN"):
            family = sockobj.getsockopt(SOL_SOCKET, SO_DOMAIN)
        if hasattr(socket, "SO_TYPE"):
            type = sockobj.getsockopt(SOL_SOCKET, SO_TYPE)
        if hasattr(socket, "SO_PROTOCOL"):
            proto = sockobj.getsockopt(SOL_SOCKET, SO_PROTOCOL)
    finally:
        # Unwrap it again, so that sockobj.__del__ doesn't try to close our socket
        sockobj.detach()
    return family, type, proto
```

And then in `fromfd` and `socket` we can do something like:

```python
family, type, proto = _fix_attrs_for_fileno(family, type, proto, fileno)
```

I have learned a little bit:

For hostname lookup, the versions of python we care about *will* automatically idna-encode unicode strings passed to `socket.getaddrinfo`, so that's nice. However, they use an old version of idna that doesn't always work.

The `idna` package on pypi supports a newer version of idna. I don't know if this always works, but requests seems to use it, so that's a vote of confidence. For hostname lookups it looks like we can just `import idna; getaddrinfo(idna.encode(...), ...)` and it should work.

For SSL hostname verification...... we certainly can't assume that stdlib ssl will do the right thing. (I guess at least it fails closed?) Before making progress on this, I'd like to summarize the discussion which happen in the past.

- [OAuth2.0 RFC6749 working group](https://www.ietf.org/mail-archive/web/oauth/current/msg02127.html) decided to adopt 400 for error HTTP status instead of 401, cuz it requires `WWW-Authenticate` to be set, and it's not always possible in all errors responses.
- @asteinlein proposed a PR at https://github.com/oauthlib/oauthlib/pull/247 to change oauthlib's errors from 400 to 401, because _some clients seem to expect 401 for API calls related to authorization_. While it had to be true, we don't know which clients were broken.
- Several bugs popup in 3rd parties librairies https://github.com/oauthjs/angular-oauth2/issues/31 https://github.com/oauthjs/angular-oauth2/issues/32#issuecomment-88888755 https://github.com/oauthjs/angular-oauth2/issues/36 . Issues are all closed now.
- The overall consensus of the current thread, and also [RFC6749#5.2](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6749#section-5.2), is that all `401` in oauthlib must be `400`. However, we have two cases with `401`:

1.  `invalid_client`, where it **MUST** be `401` if HTTP authentication is used, else it **MAY** be `401`.
2. `invalid_token`, where it **SHOULD** be `401`.

In any cases, if `401` is used, `WWW-Authenticate` **MUST** be set ([RFC2616](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc2616.html#section-10.4.2)).

### Other OAuth2.0 implementations

A quick overview of the most common OAuth2.0 implementations are similar to the overall consensus:
- Google returns `400` [so-question](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/13871982/unable-to-refresh-access-token-response-is-unauthorized-client) / [so-question](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/18572202/google-oauth2-unauthorized-client-by-refresh-token).
- Salesforce returns `400` [salesforce-forum](https://developer.salesforce.com/forums/?id=906F000000099liIAA).
- Azur returns `401` with `WWW-Authenticate` [azur-docs](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/develop/active-directory-protocols-oauth-code#error-response-2).
- AWS returns `400` [aws-cognito-docs](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cognito/latest/developerguide/token-endpoint.html). They broke the "**MUST**" of the RFC to be `401` when HTTP authentication is used.


### Conlusion
We have to align with the RFC, however to not break any implementations, we must not include these changes in 2.x.x version, but wait until 3.x.x

It depends on the size of the DataFrame. What I am trying to say is setting `DEFAULT_NPARTITIONS` is orthogonal to the use of `DEFAULT_NPARTITIONS`. We should not blindly use `DEFAULT_NPARTITIONS` when we partition the DataFrames.

The minimum size of a partition should be ~5-10MB, but, again, this is orthogonal to the `DEFAULT_NPARTITIONS` question. The overhead of deploying a task becomes small enough compared to the cost of any operation on a partition of that size that it becomes negligible. We should not limit the performance of the system for large files based on our observations for small files.
